questions.question: Who was the first ruler of the Maurya Empire?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya founded the Maurya Empire around 321 BCE with the help of Chanakya (Kautilya).

questions.question: Who wrote 'Arthashastra', a treatise on statecraft and economic policy?
questions.explanation: Arthashastra was written by Chanakya (Kautilya), the chief minister of Chandragupta Maurya. It is an ancient Indian treatise on statecraft, economic policy, and military strategy.

questions.question: The Buddhist councils were held to:
options: Compile, recite, and standardise Buddhist scriptures
questions.explanation: Four major Buddhist Councils: 1st at Rajgriha (483 BCE), 2nd at Vaishali (383 BCE), 3rd at Pataliputra (250 BCE, Ashoka's reign), 4th at Kundalvana, Kashmir (1st century CE, Kanishka's reign).

questions.question: Which Buddhist council was held during the reign of Ashoka?
questions.explanation: The 3rd Buddhist Council was held at Pataliputra (c. 250 BCE) under the patronage of Emperor Ashoka, presided over by Moggaliputta Tissa. The Pali Canon (Tipitaka) was finalised here.

questions.question: Chandragupta Maurya established the Mauryan Empire in:
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya founded the Mauryan Empire in 321 BCE after defeating the last Nanda king (Dhana Nanda) with the help of Chanakya. He made Pataliputra (modern Patna) his capital.

questions.question: Chanakya (Kautilya) authored:
questions.explanation: Chanakya (also Kautilya or Vishnugupta) wrote the Arthashastra — a comprehensive treatise on statecraft, economics, military strategy, and diplomacy. It is sometimes called 'the world's first political science text'.

questions.question: The Greek ambassador Megasthenes visited the court of:
questions.explanation: Megasthenes (c. 350–290 BCE) was the Greek ambassador sent by Seleucus I Nicator to the court of Chandragupta Maurya. His account 'Indica' (mostly lost, known through fragments) described Mauryan India.

questions.question: Ashoka's policy of Dhamma was inspired by:
options: Buddhism (after the Kalinga War in 261 BCE)
questions.explanation: The Kalinga War (261 BCE, 100,000 killed, 150,000 displaced) deeply affected Ashoka. He embraced Buddhism, renounced war, and adopted a policy of Dhamma — moral governance, non-violence, tolerance, and welfare.

questions.question: Ashoka's edicts are found in which scripts?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's edicts are inscribed in: Brahmi script (most of India), Kharosthi (northwest — modern Pakistan/Afghanistan), Aramaic (Afghanistan), and Greek (Kandahar). Content is in Pali, Prakrit, Aramaic, and Greek.

questions.question: The Mauryan Empire had its capital at:
questions.explanation: Pataliputra (modern Patna, Bihar) was the capital of the Mauryan Empire. Megasthenes described it as a magnificent city surrounded by a wooden wall with 64 gates and 570 towers.

questions.question: The last Mauryan emperor was:
questions.explanation: Brihadratha was the last Mauryan emperor. He was assassinated by his commander-in-chief Pushyamitra Shunga in 185 BCE, ending the Mauryan dynasty and starting the Shunga dynasty.

questions.question: The famous rock-cut architecture of the Mauryan period includes:
options: Barabar Caves (Bihar) — earliest rock-cut caves in India
questions.explanation: The Barabar Caves (Bihar) are the earliest surviving rock-cut caves in India, built during Ashoka's reign (c. 3rd century BCE) for the Ajivika sect. They have highly polished interiors (Mauryan polish).

questions.question: The Shunga dynasty was founded by:
questions.explanation: Pushyamitra Shunga (185–149 BCE) founded the Shunga dynasty after assassinating Brihadratha, the last Mauryan emperor. He was a Brahmin and reversed some of Ashoka's Buddhist policies.

questions.question: Chandragupta Maurya overthrew the Nanda dynasty with the help of:
questions.explanation: Chanakya (Kautilya), the brilliant political strategist and author of Arthashastra, helped Chandragupta Maurya overthrow the last Nanda king (Dhana Nanda) around 321 BCE.

questions.question: The Arthashastra deals with:
questions.explanation: Arthashastra (Science of Polity) by Kautilya covers statecraft, economic policy, military strategy, welfare state, espionage, law, and diplomacy.

questions.question: Kalinga was captured by Ashoka in:
questions.explanation: Ashoka conquered Kalinga (modern Odisha) in 261 BCE (8th year of his reign), 13 years after becoming king. The massive bloodshed transformed him.

questions.question: The term 'Dhamma' used by Ashoka was derived from:
questions.explanation: Dhamma is the Pali equivalent of Sanskrit 'Dharma'. Ashoka used Pali and Prakrit languages in his edicts to make them accessible to common people.

questions.question: The Megasthenes was the Greek ambassador at the court of:
questions.explanation: Megasthenes was sent as ambassador of Seleucus I Nicator to the court of Chandragupta Maurya. His book 'Indica' gives valuable information about Mauryan India.

questions.question: The Mauryan Empire declined after Ashoka mainly because of:
questions.explanation: After Ashoka's death (232 BCE), weak successors, heavy expenditure on Buddhist welfare, and weakening of military led to rapid decline. Pusyamitra Sunga killed last Mauryan king in 185 BCE.

questions.question: Ashokan pillars are made of:
questions.explanation: Ashokan pillars are made of a single piece of highly polished Chunar sandstone. The polish is so fine that they look like metal — technique still not fully understood.

questions.question: The Constituent Assembly adopted the National Flag on:
questions.explanation: The National Flag of India was adopted by the Constituent Assembly on 22 July 1947, three weeks before independence. The horizontal tricolor with Ashoka Chakra replaced the INC flag.

questions.question: Ashoka is referred to by name in which edict?
questions.explanation: The name 'Asoka' appears explicitly in the Maski (Raichur, Karnataka) and Gujarra (MP) minor rock edicts. In most edicts, he refers to himself as 'Devanampiya Piyadassi' (beloved of gods, Piyadassi).

questions.question: Which Pandya king is mentioned in the Ashokan edicts as a southern border king?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's Rock Edict II mentions 'the Pandyas' as southern neighbors where Ashoka's dharma principles were proclaimed. The Pandya king is not named but shows Mauryan awareness of southern kingdoms.

questions.question: The Pandya kingdom's capital was:
questions.explanation: The Pandya kingdom's capital was Madurai (also called 'Koodal'). Pandyas ruled southern Tamil Nadu from Madurai. Megasthenes mentions Pandyas ruled by a woman (queen). They traded with Greeks and Romans.

questions.question: The Kalinga State (modern Odisha) is associated with which ancient text?
questions.explanation: The Hathigumpha (Elephant Cave) inscription at Udayagiri, Odisha records the deeds of King Kharavela of Kalinga (1st century BCE), the greatest ruler of ancient Kalinga.

questions.question: Kautilya's Arthashastra was rediscovered by:
questions.explanation: R. Shamasastri (a librarian at Mysore Government Oriental Library) discovered a manuscript of Arthashastra in 1905 and published the first translation in 1909.

questions.question: Who was the founder of the Mauryan dynasty?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya founded the Mauryan dynasty with the help of Chanakya.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor converted to Buddhism after the Kalinga War?
questions.explanation: Ashoka converted to Buddhism after the Kalinga War.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan emperor who ruled after Ashoka?
questions.explanation: Dasaratha ruled after Ashoka.

questions.question: Which of the following was a key feature of the Mauryan administrative system?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan administrative system was characterized by a centralized bureaucracy.

questions.question: Who was the Mauryan emperor that expanded the empire into Central Asia?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya expanded the empire into Central Asia.

questions.question: What was the primary source of revenue for the Mauryan empire?
questions.explanation: Agriculture was the primary source of revenue for the Mauryan empire.

questions.question: Which of the following was a major consequence of the Mauryan empire's expansion?
questions.explanation: The spread of Buddhism was a major consequence of the Mauryan empire's expansion.

questions.question: Who was the Mauryan emperor that built the famous Ashoka Pillar?
questions.explanation: Ashoka built the famous Ashoka Pillar.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan capital city?
questions.explanation: Pataliputra was the Mauryan capital city.

questions.question: Which of the following was a key aspect of Mauryan foreign policy?
questions.explanation: Diplomacy was a key aspect of Mauryan foreign policy.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor is credited with the construction of the Sanchi Stupa?
questions.explanation: Ashoka built the Sanchi Stupa, a famous Buddhist monument. It is a testament to his patronage of Buddhism.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan administrative division that referred to a group of villages?
questions.explanation: Vishaya was the Mauryan administrative division for a group of villages. It was part of the hierarchical system.

questions.question: Which of the following was a key feature of the Mauryan economy?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan economy was primarily agriculture-based, with a strong emphasis on irrigation and land revenue.

questions.question: Who was the Mauryan emperor that converted to Buddhism after the Kalinga War?
questions.explanation: Ashoka converted to Buddhism after the Kalinga War, which had a profound impact on his life and reign.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan council of ministers that advised the emperor on important matters?
questions.explanation: The Mantri Parishad was the council of ministers in the Mauryan administration, responsible for advising the emperor.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor is known for his military conquests and expansion of the empire?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya is known for his military conquests, which led to the expansion of the Mauryan Empire.

questions.question: Who was the Greek ambassador to the court of Chandragupta Maurya?
questions.explanation: Megasthenes was the Greek ambassador to the court of Chandragupta Maurya, providing valuable insights into the Mauryan court.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan official responsible for the administration of justice?
questions.explanation: The Dharmamahamatra was the Mauryan official responsible for the administration of justice, ensuring the rule of law.

questions.question: Who was the founder of the Mauryan Empire?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya founded the Mauryan Empire with the help of Chanakya. He defeated the Nanda dynasty to establish his rule.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler is known for his conversion to Buddhism?
questions.explanation: Ashoka converted to Buddhism after the Kalinga War. He became a great patron of the religion.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan administrative division that referred to a province?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan Empire was divided into provinces called Bhukti. Each Bhukti was further divided into smaller units.

questions.question: Who was the Mauryan ruler that expanded the empire into South India?
questions.explanation: Bindusara expanded the Mauryan Empire into South India. He was the son of Chandragupta Maurya.

questions.question: What was the name of the famous Mauryan trade and trade route?
questions.explanation: The Uttarapatha was a major trade route during the Mauryan period. It connected the eastern and western parts of the empire.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan council of ministers?
questions.explanation: The Mantri Parishad was the council of ministers during the Mauryan period. It advised the emperor on important matters.

questions.question: Which of the following was a major consequence of the Kalinga War?
questions.explanation: The Kalinga War led to the conversion of Ashoka to Buddhism. He was moved by the suffering and bloodshed during the war.

questions.question: Who was the last major Mauryan ruler?
questions.explanation: Brihadratha was the last major Mauryan ruler. He was assassinated by his general Pushyamitra Shunga.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor patronized Buddhism and convened the Third Buddhist Council?
questions.explanation: Ashoka patronized Buddhism after the Kalinga War. He convened the Third Buddhist Council in 250 BCE.

questions.question: Who was the Mauryan emperor that expanded the empire into Central Asia and beyond?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya expanded the empire into Central Asia and beyond through his military campaigns.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan emperor who succeeded Ashoka?
questions.explanation: Dasaratha succeeded Ashoka as the Mauryan emperor.

questions.question: What was the purpose of the Mauryan edicts?
options: To proclaim the emperor's military victories
To outline the empire's administrative policies
questions.explanation: The Mauryan edicts were used to spread Buddhism and outline the empire's administrative policies.

questions.question: Who was the Mauryan emperor that faced a major rebellion in Taxila?
questions.explanation: Ashoka faced a major rebellion in Taxila during his reign.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan official responsible for collecting taxes?
questions.explanation: Samaharta was the Mauryan official responsible for collecting taxes.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan emperor who defeated Seleucus Nicator?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya defeated Seleucus Nicator and gained control of the northwestern part of the Indian subcontinent.

questions.question: Who was the Mauryan emperor that built the famous Ashokan edicts?
questions.explanation: Ashoka built the famous Ashokan edicts, which showcased his policies and reforms.

questions.question: Which of the following Mauryan emperors is known for his military conquests?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya is known for his military conquests, which helped establish the Mauryan empire.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan official responsible for tax collection?
questions.explanation: The Samaharta was the Mauryan official responsible for tax collection.

questions.question: Who was the Greek ambassador to the Mauryan court during the reign of Chandragupta Maurya?
questions.explanation: Megasthenes was the Greek ambassador to the Mauryan court during the reign of Chandragupta Maurya.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor is credited with the spread of Buddhism throughout the Indian subcontinent?
questions.explanation: Ashoka played a significant role in spreading Buddhism after his conversion. He sent Buddhist missionaries to various parts of the world.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor is known for his military conquests?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya is known for his extensive military conquests that helped establish the Mauryan Empire.

questions.question: Who was the Mauryan emperor that converted to Buddhism after a war?
questions.explanation: Ashoka converted to Buddhism after the Kalinga War, which had a profound impact on his life and reign.

questions.question: What was the significance of the Kalinga War in Mauryan history?
options: It marked the beginning of the Mauryan Empire
It led to the conquest of South India
It resulted in Ashoka's conversion to Buddhism
questions.explanation: The Kalinga War was a turning point in Ashoka's life, leading to his conversion to Buddhism and a change in his policies.

questions.question: Who was the Mauryan emperor that expanded the empire into southern India?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya expanded the empire into southern India.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan council of ministers that advised the emperor?
questions.explanation: Mantri Parishad was the council of ministers in the Mauryan administration.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor issued the Rock Edicts and Pillar Edicts?
questions.explanation: Ashoka issued the Rock Edicts and Pillar Edicts.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan Emperor who converted to Buddhism?
questions.explanation: Ashoka converted to Buddhism after the Kalinga War. He became a great patron of Buddhism.

questions.question: Which Mauryan Emperor is known for his military campaigns and expansion of the empire?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya expanded the Mauryan Empire through his military campaigns. He conquered much of ancient India.

questions.question: What was the capital of the Mauryan Empire?
questions.explanation: Pataliputra was the capital of the Mauryan Empire. It was an important center of trade and culture.

questions.question: Who was the Mauryan Emperor who built the famous Ashokan pillars?
questions.explanation: Ashoka built the Ashokan pillars to spread Buddhism. These pillars have inscriptions of Ashoka's edicts.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan Emperor who wrote the Arthashastra?
questions.explanation: Chanakya wrote the Arthashastra. It is a treatise on statecraft and economics.

questions.question: Which of the following was a major feature of the Mauryan administration?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan administration was centralized. The emperor held supreme power and authority.

questions.question: Who was the last Mauryan Emperor?
questions.explanation: Brihadratha was the last Mauryan Emperor. He was overthrown by Pushyamitra Shunga.

questions.question: What was the name of the battle that led to the conversion of Ashoka to Buddhism?
questions.explanation: The Kalinga War led to the conversion of Ashoka to Buddhism. He was moved by the suffering and bloodshed.

questions.question: Which of the following was a major consequence of the Mauryan Empire's decline?
questions.explanation: The decline of the Mauryan Empire led to the rise of the Gupta Empire. The Guptas filled the power vacuum left by the Mauryans.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan administrative division that referred to a province or region?
questions.explanation: Bhukti was a Mauryan administrative division. It referred to a province or region.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan official responsible for collecting taxes and maintaining law and order?
questions.explanation: Rajuka was the Mauryan official responsible for collecting taxes and maintaining law and order. He played a crucial role in local administration.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan era that marked the beginning of the Buddhist calendar?
questions.explanation: Ashokan era marked the beginning of the Buddhist calendar. It started from Ashoka's coronation.

questions.question: Who was the Mauryan emperor that expanded the empire into Central Asia and the Deccan?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya expanded the empire into Central Asia and the Deccan. He conquered the Nanda dynasty and established the Mauryan empire.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan official responsible for collecting taxes and tributes?
questions.explanation: Samaharta was the Mauryan official responsible for collecting taxes and tributes. He played a crucial role in the administrative system.

questions.question: Which of the following was a major consequence of the Kalinga war for the Mauryan empire?
questions.explanation: The Kalinga war led to the rise of Buddhism in the Mauryan empire. Ashoka converted to Buddhism after the war.

questions.question: Which of the following was a major feature of the Mauryan economy?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan economy was primarily agriculture-based. The empire relied heavily on agriculture for its revenue.

questions.question: Who was the Mauryan emperor that built the famous Sanchi Stupa?
questions.explanation: Ashoka built the famous Sanchi Stupa. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and a major Buddhist monument.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan administrative division that referred to a village?
questions.explanation: Gram was the Mauryan administrative division that referred to a village. It was the smallest unit of administration.

questions.question: Who was the Mauryan Emperor that ruled after Ashoka?
questions.explanation: Dasaratha succeeded Ashoka as the Mauryan Emperor. He ruled for a short period.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan administrative division?
questions.explanation: Janapada was the administrative division of the Mauryan Empire. It was a territorial unit.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan Emperor who issued the largest number of edicts?
questions.explanation: Ashoka issued the largest number of edicts. His edicts were inscribed on rocks and pillars.

questions.question: Which of the following was a consequence of the Kalinga War?
questions.explanation: The Kalinga War led to the conversion of Ashoka to Buddhism. He was moved by the suffering and bloodshed in the war.

questions.question: Which of the following was a major feature of the Mauryan administrative system?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan administrative system was highly centralized with the emperor holding supreme power. The empire was divided into provinces for administrative convenience.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan emperor who expanded the empire through conquest?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya expanded the Mauryan Empire through conquest with the help of Chanakya. He defeated several kingdoms to establish his empire.

questions.question: Which of the following was a major economic activity during the Mauryan period?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan economy was diverse with agriculture, trade, and industry being major economic activities. The empire was known for its extensive trade networks.

questions.question: Who was the Mauryan emperor known for his military campaigns?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya was known for his military campaigns that expanded the Mauryan Empire. He conquered several kingdoms to establish his empire.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan administrative division below the province?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan empire was divided into provinces called Bhukti, which were further divided into smaller administrative units. The Bhukti was an important administrative division.

questions.question: Which of the following was a major social feature of the Mauryan period?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan period saw the existence of a rigid caste system with four varnas. The caste system played an important role in Mauryan society.

questions.question: Who was the Mauryan emperor known for his diplomatic relations with neighboring kingdoms?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya was known for his diplomatic relations with neighboring kingdoms, including the Seleucid Empire. He maintained good relations with neighboring kingdoms to secure his empire.

questions.question: Who was the Mauryan emperor that faced a rebellion in Taxila?
questions.explanation: Susima faced a rebellion in Taxila during his reign. The rebellion was eventually put down.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor built the famous Ashoka Pillar at Sarnath?
questions.explanation: Ashoka built the Ashoka Pillar at Sarnath to commemorate his conversion to Buddhism. The pillar features four lions.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan emperor that ruled after Ashoka?
questions.explanation: Dasaratha ruled the Mauryan Empire after Ashoka. He was the grandson of Ashoka.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan emperor who converted to Buddhism after the Kalinga War?
questions.explanation: Ashoka converted to Buddhism after the Kalinga War. He became a patron of Buddhism.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan emperor who wrote the book 'Arthashastra'?
questions.explanation: Kautilya wrote the book 'Arthashastra', a treatise on statecraft and economics. It is a key source of information on the Mauryan empire.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan emperor who died under mysterious circumstances?
questions.explanation: Dasaratha died under mysterious circumstances, with some accounts suggesting he was poisoned. The exact circumstances of his death are not known.

questions.question: What was the primary purpose of the Mauryan edicts?
questions.explanation: The primary purpose of the Mauryan edicts was to spread Buddhism and Ashoka's teachings. They were inscribed on rocks and pillars.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan council that advised the emperor on administrative matters?
questions.explanation: The Mantri Parishad was the council that advised the Mauryan emperor on administrative matters. It played a crucial role in governance.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan administrative division that was headed by a governor?
questions.explanation: Bhukti was the administrative division of the Mauryan empire. It was headed by a governor.

questions.question: The Mauryan empire's decline is often attributed to which of the following factors?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan empire declined due to a combination of factors. These include overextension, economic troubles, and weak successors.

questions.question: The Edicts of Ashoka were inscribed on which of the following?
questions.explanation: The Edicts of Ashoka were inscribed on pillars. These pillars were erected throughout the Mauryan empire.

questions.question: The Mauryan empire's capital city was located at which of the following sites?
questions.explanation: Pataliputra was the capital city of the Mauryan empire. It was an important center of politics and culture.

questions.question: Which of the following was a key feature of the Mauryan system of government?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan system of government was characterized by centralization. This meant that power was concentrated in the hands of the emperor.

questions.question: The Mauryan empire was known for its extensive network of which of the following?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan empire had an extensive network of roads. These roads facilitated trade and communication.

questions.question: What was the primary reason for the decline of the Mauryan empire?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan empire declined due to weak succession and internal conflicts. Weak succession led to division and instability.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor is credited with the spread of Buddhism?
questions.explanation: Ashoka played a significant role in spreading Buddhism throughout his empire after the Kalinga War.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan administrative division that referred to a province or territory?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan empire was divided into provinces or territories known as Janapadas or Bhuktis for administrative purposes.

questions.question: Who founded the Mauryan Empire?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya founded the Mauryan Empire around 321 BCE with the guidance of Chanakya.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler was known as 'Devanampiya' and 'Piyadassi'?
questions.explanation: Ashoka was called 'Devanampiya' (Beloved of the Gods) and 'Piyadassi' (Gracious of Mien) in his inscriptions.

questions.question: Who wrote the Arthashastra, a key text on Mauryan statecraft?
questions.explanation: Chanakya (also known as Kautilya) authored the Arthashastra, detailing Mauryan administration and policy.

questions.question: In which script were most of Ashoka's edicts inscribed?
questions.explanation: The majority of Ashoka's rock and pillar edicts were written in the Brahmi script.

questions.question: Which Greek ambassador visited the court of Chandragupta Maurya?
questions.explanation: Megasthenes, a Greek envoy from Seleucus Nicator, visited Chandragupta Maurya's court and wrote 'Indica'.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler was known as 'Amitraghata' (slayer of enemies)?
questions.explanation: Bindusara, son of Chandragupta, was called 'Amitraghata' meaning slayer of enemies.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler built the famous Lion Capital at Sarnath?
questions.explanation: Ashoka erected the Lion Capital of Sarnath around 250 BCE, now the national emblem of India.

questions.question: How many provinces was the Mauryan Empire divided into for administration?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan Empire was divided into four provinces: Uttarapatha, Avantipatha, Prachyapatha, and Dakshinapatha.

questions.question: In which year did Ashoka conquer Kalinga?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's conquest of Kalinga occurred in 261 BCE, after which he embraced Buddhism.

questions.question: Which war led Ashoka to embrace Buddhism?
questions.explanation: The Kalinga War (261 BCE) caused massive bloodshed, leading Ashoka to adopt non-violence and Buddhism.

questions.question: Who was the last ruler of the Mauryan Empire?
questions.explanation: Brihadratha was the last Mauryan ruler, assassinated by his commander Pushyamitra Shunga in 185 BCE.

questions.question: Which famous Mauryan text was written by Chanakya?
questions.explanation: Arthashastra, a treatise on statecraft and economics, was authored by Chanakya (Kautilya).

questions.question: What was the official language used in Ashokan edicts?
questions.explanation: Most Ashokan edicts were inscribed in Prakrit language using Brahmi script.

questions.question: Which Mauryan king was known as 'Devanampriya Priyadarshi'?
questions.explanation: Ashoka referred to himself as 'Devanampriya Priyadarshi' (Beloved of the Gods) in his edicts.

questions.question: Which animal is depicted on the national emblem of India adopted from Ashoka?
questions.explanation: The Lion Capital of Ashoka at Sarnath features four lions and is the national emblem of India.

questions.question: What was the administrative division below the 'province' (Pradesha) in the Mauryan Empire?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan Empire was divided into provinces (Pradesha), further subdivided into districts (Ahar).

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler embraced Buddhism after the Kalinga War?
questions.explanation: Ashoka converted to Buddhism following the bloody Kalinga War in 261 BCE.

questions.question: The Mauryan administration was highly centralized. Who was the head of the empire?
questions.explanation: The King was the supreme head of the Mauryan state, assisted by a council of ministers.

questions.question: Which book by Chanakya provides detailed information on Mauryan statecraft and administration?
questions.explanation: Kautilya's Arthashastra is a treatise on politics, economics, and military strategy of the Mauryan period.

questions.question: What was the main source of revenue for the Mauryan Empire?
questions.explanation: Land tax (Bhaga), usually one-sixth of the produce, was the primary revenue source.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor was known as 'Amitraghata' (slayer of enemies)?
questions.explanation: Bindusara, son of Chandragupta, was called Amitraghata by Greek sources.

questions.question: The famous Lion Capital of Ashoka is located at which place?
questions.explanation: The Lion Capital at Sarnath is the national emblem of India and was built by Ashoka.

questions.question: Who was the last ruler of the Mauryan dynasty?
questions.explanation: Brihadratha was the last Mauryan ruler, assassinated by his commander Pushyamitra Shunga in 185 BCE.

questions.question: Who was the author of 'Arthashastra'?
questions.explanation: Chanakya, also known as Kautilya, wrote Arthashastra, a treatise on statecraft and economics.

questions.question: The famous rock edicts of Ashoka are written in which script?
questions.explanation: Majority of Ashoka's rock edicts are inscribed in Brahmi script, though Kharosthi was used in the northwest.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler was known as 'Devanampiya' and 'Piyadasi'?
questions.explanation: Ashoka used the titles 'Devanampiya' (Beloved of the Gods) and 'Piyadasi' (Pleasant to Behold) in his inscriptions.

questions.question: Where is the famous 'Lion Capital of Ashoka' located?
questions.explanation: The Lion Capital of Ashoka, now the national emblem of India, is from the pillar at Sarnath.

questions.question: What was the name of the tax collected by the Mauryan state from the farmers?
questions.explanation: Bhaga was the land tax (usually one-sixth of produce) collected from farmers during the Mauryan period.

questions.question: Which South Indian kingdoms are mentioned in Ashoka's inscriptions as friendly neighbors?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's inscriptions mention the Chera, Chola, Pandya, and Satyaputra kingdoms as independent but friendly.

questions.question: The last ruler of the Mauryan dynasty was?
questions.explanation: Brihadratha was the last Mauryan ruler, assassinated by his commander Pushyamitra Shunga.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler embraced Buddhism and promoted Dhamma?
questions.explanation: Ashoka adopted Buddhism after the Kalinga War and propagated Dhamma through edicts and pillars.

questions.question: Which war led Ashoka to renounce violence and adopt Buddhism?
questions.explanation: The brutal Kalinga War (c. 261 BCE) caused massive casualties, leading Ashoka to embrace non-violence and Buddhism.

questions.question: What was the primary source of revenue for the Mauryan state?
questions.explanation: Land tax (Bhaga), usually one-sixth of the produce, was the main source of revenue as per Kautilya's Arthashastra.

questions.question: Who wrote the Arthashastra, a key text on Mauryan administration?
questions.explanation: Chanakya (Kautilya) authored the Arthashastra, detailing statecraft, economy, and military strategy of the Mauryan period.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT a major Mauryan center of administration?
questions.explanation: Madurai was a major city of the Pandya kingdom in the far south, not part of the Mauryan provincial administration.

questions.question: What script was used in most of Ashoka's edicts?
questions.explanation: The majority of Ashokan edicts were inscribed in the Brahmi script, except in the northwest where Kharosthi was used.

questions.question: Which war led to Ashoka's conversion to Buddhism?
questions.explanation: The Kalinga War (261 BCE) caused immense bloodshed, leading Ashoka to embrace Buddhism and adopt Dhamma.

questions.question: Who authored the Arthashastra, a key source for Mauryan administration?
questions.explanation: Kautilya, also known as Chanakya, authored the Arthashastra, detailing Mauryan statecraft and economy.

questions.question: What were the provincial governors in the Mauryan administration called?
questions.explanation: Provinces were governed by princes (Kumara) or royal family members.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of Mauryan art and architecture?
questions.explanation: The Great Stupa at Sanchi was built during the Mauryan period, especially under Ashoka.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler is known for his rock and pillar edicts?
questions.explanation: Ashoka erected numerous pillars and rock edicts across his empire to propagate his Dhamma policies.

questions.question: The decline of the Mauryan Empire began after the death of which ruler?
questions.explanation: After Ashoka's death in 232 BCE, the empire weakened due to weak successors and internal revolts.

questions.question: The Mauryan Empire reached its greatest territorial extent under which ruler?
questions.explanation: Ashoka expanded the empire to cover most of the Indian subcontinent after the Kalinga War.

questions.question: What was the official language of the Mauryan inscriptions?
questions.explanation: Most Ashokan edicts were inscribed in Prakrit using the Brahmi script.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler is known as 'Amitraghata' (slayer of enemies)?
questions.explanation: Bindusara was called Amitraghata by Greek sources for his military conquests.

questions.question: The Mauryan administration had a council of ministers known as?
questions.explanation: The Mantriparishad, headed by the king, advised on state affairs as per Kautilya's Arthashastra.

questions.question: Which Tamil Sangam text mentions trade with the Mauryan Empire?
questions.explanation: Purananuru refers to Mauryan chariots and trade links with the Tamil region.

questions.question: The famous Edicts of Ashoka are inscribed in which script primarily?
questions.explanation: Most Ashokan edicts use Brahmi script, while some in the northwest use Kharosthi.

questions.question: Which Tamil king is mentioned in the edicts of Ashoka?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's Rock Edict II refers to the Pandyan king as a southern neighbor, indicating Mauryan-Tamil contact.

questions.question: Which Tamil kingdom is mentioned in Ashoka's rock edicts?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's edicts list the Cholas, Pandyas, Satyaputras, and Keralaputras as neighboring kingdoms in the Tamil region.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler is known for his policy of Dhamma and engraving edicts on pillars and rocks?
questions.explanation: Ashoka the Great propagated Dhamma after the Kalinga War and issued edicts inscribed on pillars and rocks across his empire.

questions.question: Which of the following Tamil kingdoms is mentioned in Ashoka's Major Rock Edict II as a border kingdom?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's Major Rock Edict II mentions Cholas, Pandyas, Satyaputras, and Keralaputras (Cheras) as border kingdoms.

questions.question: The famous 'Lion Capital' of Ashoka is from which site?
questions.explanation: The Lion Capital of Ashoka was erected at Sarnath, Uttar Pradesh, and is now the national emblem of India.

questions.question: Who wrote the 'Arthashastra', a key source for Mauryan administration?
questions.explanation: Kautilya (Chanakya) authored the Arthashastra, a treatise on statecraft and Mauryan administration.

questions.question: The Kalinga War was fought during the reign of which Mauryan ruler?
questions.explanation: Ashoka fought the Kalinga War around 261 BCE, which led to his conversion to Buddhism and adoption of Dhamma.

questions.question: The Mauryan empire declined after the assassination of the last Mauryan ruler by which general?
questions.explanation: Pushyamitra Sunga, a Brahmin general, assassinated the last Mauryan ruler Brihadratha and founded the Sunga dynasty.

questions.question: Under the Mauryan administration, the empire was divided into provinces called?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan empire was divided into provinces known as 'Pradesh' (e.g., Uttarapatha, Dakshinapatha) governed by princes or officials.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a source for Mauryan history?
questions.explanation: Rajatarangini is a 12th-century chronicle of Kashmir by Kalhana, not a source for Mauryan history.

questions.question: Ashoka's Dhamma policy included all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Ashoka discouraged animal sacrifice as part of his Dhamma, which promoted non-violence and compassion.

questions.question: The Mauryan emperor who embraced Jainism and later starved himself to death at Shravanabelagola?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya adopted Jainism, abdicated the throne, and fasted unto death at Shravanabelagola in Karnataka.

questions.question: Who founded the Maurya dynasty?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya founded the Mauryan empire in 322 BCE with the guidance of Chanakya.

questions.question: Who was the last Mauryan ruler?
questions.explanation: Brihadratha was the last Mauryan emperor, assassinated by his commander Pushyamitra Sunga in 185 BCE.

questions.question: The Lion Capital of Ashoka, now the national emblem of India, was originally erected at which site?
questions.explanation: The Lion Capital was erected at Sarnath by Ashoka to commemorate the first sermon of Buddha.

questions.question: Which Major Rock Edict of Ashoka describes the Kalinga war and its aftermath?
questions.explanation: Major Rock Edict XIII details Ashoka's remorse after the Kalinga war and his adoption of Dhamma.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler's edicts mention the Cholas, Pandyas, and other Tamil kingdoms?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's Major Rock Edicts II and XIII refer to the Cholas, Pandyas, and other neighboring Tamil dynasties.

questions.question: Into how many major provinces was the Mauryan empire divided for administrative convenience?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan empire had five major provinces: Magadha, Taxila, Ujjain, Tosali, and Suvarnagiri.

questions.question: Which ancient text provides detailed information about Mauryan administration and economy?
questions.explanation: Kautilya's Arthashastra is a treatise on statecraft that describes Mauryan governance, economy, and military.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler is believed to have embraced Jainism and ended his life by fasting?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya renounced his throne, became a Jain monk, and starved to death at Shravanabelagola.

questions.question: Which of the following is a distinctive feature of Mauryan sculpture?
questions.explanation: Mauryan pillars are known for their high polish (Mauryan polish) and monolithic design, as seen in Ashoka's pillars.

questions.question: The famous Kalinga War was fought in which year?
questions.explanation: The Kalinga War (261 BCE) led Ashoka to embrace Buddhism and adopt Dhamma policy.

questions.question: Which Ashokan edict mentions the Greek kings as contemporaries?
questions.explanation: Major Rock Edict XIII lists Antiochos, Ptolemy, Antigonos, Magas, and Alexander as contemporary rulers.

questions.question: Which Ashokan edict mentions the Cholas, Pandyas, Satyaputras, and Keralaputras?
questions.explanation: Major Rock Edict II records the southern kingdoms as part of Ashoka's Dhamma outreach.

questions.question: The Arthashastra, a treatise on statecraft, was written by:
questions.explanation: Chanakya (Kautilya) authored the Arthashastra, a key source for Mauryan administration.

questions.question: The Mauryan Empire began to decline after the death of which ruler?
questions.explanation: After Ashoka's death in 232 BCE, the empire fragmented due to weak successors and invasions.

questions.question: Which Mauryan king is associated with the spread of Buddhism to Sri Lanka?
questions.explanation: Ashoka sent his son Mahinda and daughter Sanghamitta to Sri Lanka to propagate Buddhism.

questions.question: Ashoka's Kalinga war took place in which year?
questions.explanation: The Kalinga war in 261 BCE (Ashoka's 8th regnal year) led to his conversion to Buddhism.

questions.question: Which rock edict of Ashoka mentions the Greek rulers Antiyoka, Turamaya, etc.?
questions.explanation: Major Rock Edict XIII lists Hellenistic kings as recipients of Ashoka's Dhamma propagation.

questions.question: The Lion Capital of Ashoka was adopted as the national emblem of India. It is from which site?
questions.explanation: The Lion Capital was discovered at Sarnath, Uttar Pradesh, on a pillar erected by Ashoka.

questions.question: Which Mauryan king was known as 'Amitraghata' (slayer of enemies)?
questions.explanation: Bindusara, son of Chandragupta, was called Amitraghata in Greek sources for his military prowess.

questions.question: Which Tamil Kingdom sent an embassy to Ashoka's court according to the Second Rock Edict?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's Major Rock Edict II mentions the Cholas, Pandyas, Satyaputras, and Keralaputras as friendly border kingdoms.

questions.question: The famous Mauryan art monument 'Stupa' at Sanchi was originally built by:
questions.explanation: The Great Stupa at Sanchi was initially constructed by Ashoka and later enlarged during the Shunga period.

questions.question: Which work by Chanakya (Kautilya) provides details about Mauryan administration?
questions.explanation: Kautilya's Arthashastra is a treatise on statecraft, detailing Mauryan fiscal, military, and civil administration.

questions.question: How many major provinces (viceroyalties) did the Mauryan administration have?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan empire was divided into four provinces with capitals at Tosali, Ujjain, Suvarnagiri, and Taxila.

questions.question: Which Major Rock Edict of Ashoka describes the horrors of the Kalinga war?
questions.explanation: Major Rock Edict 13 narrates the Kalinga war and Ashoka's remorse, leading to his embrace of Buddhism.

questions.question: Which Mauryan art piece is the national emblem of India?
questions.explanation: The Sarnath Lion Capital, erected by Ashoka, was adopted as the national emblem of India.

questions.question: With which Hellenistic kingdom did the Mauryans maintain diplomatic relations?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya made a treaty with Seleucus Nicator, and Megasthenes was the Greek ambassador.

questions.question: Which Tamil Sangam text mentions a Mauryan invasion of the Tamil region?
questions.explanation: Purananuru refers to the invasion of 'Morias' (Mauryans) and the resistance by Tamil chieftains.

questions.question: In Mauryan administration, the officer 'Samaharta' was primarily responsible for?
questions.explanation: Samaharta was the revenue collector in the Mauryan administrative system as per Kautilya's Arthashastra.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler is most associated with the spread of Buddhism across Asia?
questions.explanation: Ashoka sent Buddhist missions to Sri Lanka, Southeast Asia, and the Hellenistic world after the Kalinga war.

questions.question: What was the administrative unit during Mauryan times headed by a 'Rashtrapa'?
questions.explanation: The empire was divided into provinces called Rashtras, each under a Rashtrapa.

questions.question: Which source describes the administration of Pataliputra during Mauryan times?
questions.explanation: Kautilya's Arthashastra details Mauryan statecraft and the administration of Pataliputra.

questions.question: The Kalinga War is mentioned in which Ashokan edict?
questions.explanation: Rock Edict XIII describes the Kalinga War and its impact on Ashoka's conversion.

questions.question: Which Mauryan king is associated with the title 'Devanampriya'?
questions.explanation: Ashoka used the title 'Devanampriya' (Beloved of the Gods) in his inscriptions.

questions.question: Who succeeded Chandragupta Maurya as the Mauryan emperor?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta was succeeded by his son Bindusara, who ruled from 298 to 273 BCE.

questions.question: Which port was a major trade center in the Mauryan Empire?
questions.explanation: Tamralipti (modern Tamluk) was a prominent Mauryan port for trade with Southeast Asia.

questions.question: The Edicts of Ashoka were inscribed in which scripts?
questions.explanation: Most Ashokan edicts used Brahmi script; Kharosthi was used in the northwestern regions.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler embraced Buddhism and actively spread it?
questions.explanation: After the Kalinga War, Ashoka converted to Buddhism and promoted Dhamma across the empire.

questions.question: What was the council of ministers in Mauryan administration called?
questions.explanation: The Mantriparishad was the council of ministers that advised the Mauryan king on governance.

questions.question: Who wrote the Arthashastra and served as a minister under Chandragupta Maurya?
questions.explanation: Chanakya (also known as Kautilya) authored the Arthashastra, a treatise on statecraft and economics.

questions.question: Which was the main source of revenue for the Mauryan state?
questions.explanation: Land tax (Bhaga), collected as one-sixth of the produce, was the primary revenue source.

questions.question: The famous lion capital of Ashoka, now the national emblem of India, was originally erected at which location?
questions.explanation: The lion capital was erected at Sarnath, marking the site of Buddha's first sermon.

questions.question: Which Mauryan king is best known for his policy of Dhamma?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's Dhamma was a set of ethical principles aimed at social harmony and non-violence.

questions.question: The Mauryan emperor Ashoka embraced Buddhism after which war?
questions.explanation: Ashoka embraced Buddhism after the bloody Kalinga War (c. 261 BCE), which led him to adopt the policy of Dhamma and non-violence.

questions.question: Which of the following is a major source for understanding Mauryan administration?
questions.explanation: Chanakya's Arthashastra is a comprehensive treatise on statecraft, economics, and military strategy that provides detailed insights into Mauryan administration.

questions.question: The Mauryan Empire's capital was located at which city?
questions.explanation: Pataliputra (modern Patna) served as the capital of the Mauryan Empire, strategically located at the confluence of the Ganges and Son rivers.

questions.question: Which of the following South Indian kingdoms is mentioned in Ashoka's edicts?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's Rock Edict II mentions the Chera, Chola, Pandya, and Satyaputra kingdoms as frontier states in South India.

questions.question: What was the primary script used in Ashoka's major rock edicts?
questions.explanation: Most of Ashoka's edicts were inscribed in the Brahmi script, though the ones in the northwest used Kharosthi. Brahmi was the parent script for many later Indian scripts.

questions.question: The Mauryan emperor who was known as 'Amitraghata' (slayer of enemies) was:
questions.explanation: Bindusara, son of Chandragupta and father of Ashoka, was known by the title 'Amitraghata', meaning destroyer of foes. He extended the empire to the Deccan.

questions.question: Which of the following rock edicts of Ashoka mentions the concept of 'Dhamma' extensively?
questions.explanation: Major Rock Edict XIII describes Ashoka's remorse after the Kalinga War and his adoption of Dhamma, though the term is used in many edicts; this one is most famous for the transformation.

questions.question: The famous Mauryan 'Lion Capital' originally stood at which site?
questions.explanation: The Lion Capital of Ashoka was originally erected at Sarnath near Varanasi to mark the spot where Buddha first taught the Dharma. It is now the national emblem of India.

questions.question: Who was the founder of the Maurya dynasty?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya founded the Maurya dynasty in 322 BCE after overthrowing the Nanda dynasty.

questions.question: The Kalinga War, which led to Ashoka's conversion to Buddhism, took place in which year?
questions.explanation: The Kalinga War was fought in 261 BCE, after which Ashoka embraced Buddhism and propagated Dhamma.

questions.question: Ashoka's Rock Edict II mentions the southern kingdoms of Cholas, Pandyas, Satyaputra, and Keralaputra. In which context?
questions.explanation: Edict II states that these southern kingdoms, though not under Mauryan rule, followed Ashoka's Dhamma.

questions.question: What was the language and script used in Ashoka's pillar edicts?
questions.explanation: Majority of Ashoka's edicts were inscribed in Prakrit language using Brahmi script; Kharosthi was used in the northwest.

questions.question: Under the Mauryan administration, the empire was divided into how many provinces, each headed by a prince?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan empire had four major provinces: Northern (Taxila), Western (Ujjain), Eastern (Tosali), and Southern (Suvarnagiri).

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler is believed to have patronised the Third Buddhist Council held at Pataliputra?
questions.explanation: The Third Buddhist Council was convened under the patronage of Ashoka in 250 BCE at Pataliputra.

questions.question: The famous Sanchi Stupa was originally built during the reign of which Mauryan emperor?
questions.explanation: The Great Stupa at Sanchi was originally built by Ashoka in the 3rd century BCE and later enlarged.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor was known as 'Amitraghata' (slayer of enemies) by Greek sources?
questions.explanation: Bindusara was called Amitraghata by Greek writers; he maintained diplomatic relations with the Hellenistic world.

questions.question: The last ruler of the Maurya dynasty, who was assassinated by his general Pushyamitra Shunga, was:
questions.explanation: Brihadratha, the last Mauryan ruler, was killed by Pushyamitra Shunga in 185 BCE, ending the dynasty.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor is credited with the propagation of Dhamma through rock and pillar edicts?
questions.explanation: Ashoka, the third Mauryan emperor, issued rock and pillar edicts to spread his policy of Dhamma across the empire.

questions.question: The famous 'Lion Capital of Ashoka' was originally erected at which location?
questions.explanation: The Lion Capital of Ashoka was erected at Sarnath, Uttar Pradesh, and is now the national emblem of India.

questions.question: The administrative division 'Ahar' in the Mauryan Empire referred to which level?
questions.explanation: Ahar was a district-level administrative unit in the Mauryan Empire, often comprising several villages.

questions.question: Which Tamil Sangam text mentions the Mauryan invasion of the Tamil region?
questions.explanation: The Purananuru, a Sangam anthology, refers to the Mauryan invasion and the resistance by Tamil chieftains like the Muvendars.

questions.question: Which Mauryan king is known for building the famous 'Sudarsana Lake' in Gujarat?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya is credited with constructing the Sudarsana Lake, later repaired by the Shaka ruler Rudradaman.

questions.question: The Arthashastra, a key source for Mauryan administration, was written by whom?
questions.explanation: The Arthashastra is attributed to Chanakya, also known as Kautilya or Vishnugupta, the chief advisor of Chandragupta Maurya.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor is associated with the construction of the 'Mahastupa' at Sanchi?
questions.explanation: Ashoka built the original brick stupa at Sanchi, which was later enlarged and faced with stone during the Shunga period.

questions.question: The Mauryan Empire's capital Pataliputra was located at the confluence of which rivers?
questions.explanation: Pataliputra (modern Patna) was situated at the confluence of the Ganga and Son rivers, a strategic location.

questions.question: Which Greek ambassador is known for his detailed account of Mauryan administration?
questions.explanation: Megasthenes was the Greek ambassador sent by Seleucus to Chandragupta Maurya's court; his work 'Indica' provides valuable details on Mauryan society and administration.

questions.question: Which of the following Ashokan rock edicts mentions the names of the Chola, Pandya, Satiyaputra, and Keralaputra kingdoms?
questions.explanation: Major Rock Edict II lists the Cholas, Pandyas, Satiyaputra, and Keralaputra as border kingdoms that followed Ashoka's Dhamma.

questions.question: The Mauryan empire was divided into how many provinces for administrative convenience?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan empire was divided into four provinces – Uttarpatha, Prachya, Avantiratha, and Dakshinapatha – each under a prince or viceroy.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler was known by the title 'Amitraghata' (slayer of enemies)?
questions.explanation: Bindusara, son of Chandragupta and father of Ashoka, was known as 'Amitraghata' meaning slayer of enemies.

questions.question: The Sarnath Lion Capital, which is the national emblem of India, was erected by which emperor?
questions.explanation: The Lion Capital at Sarnath was erected by Emperor Ashoka to mark the site where Buddha first preached his teachings; it is a masterpiece of Mauryan art.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a source for the study of Mauryan history?
questions.explanation: Rajatarangini (12th century CE) is a chronicle of Kashmir kings, while the other three works provide information about the Mauryan period.

questions.question: The Mauryan emperor who abdicated the throne and became a Jain monk was?
questions.explanation: According to Jain tradition, Chandragupta Maurya abdicated the throne and spent his final years as a Jain monk in Shravanabelagola.

questions.question: Which Ashokan edict is considered the 'keystone' of Ashoka's Dhamma policy and describes his remorse after the Kalinga War?
questions.explanation: Major Rock Edict XIII directly describes the Kalinga War, the carnage, and Ashoka's subsequent conversion to Dhamma.

questions.question: The Mauryan system of taxation included a land tax known as:
questions.explanation: Bhaga was the land tax, typically one-sixth of the produce, collected by the Mauryan state as described in the Arthashastra.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler is credited with the conquest of Kalinga, which led to his adoption of Dhamma?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's Kalinga War (261 BCE) caused immense bloodshed, prompting his conversion to Buddhism and propagation of Dhamma.

questions.question: The administrative council of the Mauryan empire, consisting of high officials, was known as:
questions.explanation: The Mantriparishad was the council of ministers that advised the Mauryan king on state affairs.

questions.question: Which Greek ambassador visited the court of Chandragupta Maurya and wrote the 'Indica'?
questions.explanation: Megasthenes was sent by Seleucus Nicator to Chandragupta's court; his work 'Indica' describes Mauryan society and administration.

questions.question: The Mauryan emperor who was known as 'Amitraghata' (slayer of enemies) by Greek sources was:
questions.explanation: Bindusara, son of Chandragupta, was referred to as Amitraghata in Greek texts for his military campaigns.

questions.question: Which Mauryan official was responsible for the collection of revenue and maintenance of accounts?
questions.explanation: The Samaharta was the chief revenue collector, while the Sannidhata was the treasurer in the Mauryan administration.

questions.question: The famous Mauryan pillar edict at Lauriya Nandangarh is located in which modern Indian state?
questions.explanation: Lauriya Nandangarh, in Bihar's West Champaran district, houses a polished sandstone pillar with Ashokan edicts.

questions.question: Which Tamil Sangam work mentions trade with the Mauryan empire?
questions.explanation: The Purananuru (poem 175) refers to Mauryan chariots and trade links between Tamil chieftains and the northern empire.

questions.question: The Mauryan art and architecture style is best exemplified by which surviving monument?
questions.explanation: The Great Stupa at Sanchi, originally built by Ashoka, is a classic example of Mauryan brick and stone architecture.

questions.question: Which Mauryan text, written by Kautilya, details statecraft, economy, and military strategy?
questions.explanation: Kautilya's Arthashastra is a comprehensive treatise on governance, taxation, and espionage during the Mauryan period.

questions.question: The last Mauryan emperor, who was assassinated by his commander Pushyamitra Shunga, was:
questions.explanation: Brihadratha, the final Mauryan ruler, was killed in 185 BCE by Pushyamitra Shunga, who founded the Shunga dynasty.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler was responsible for the conquest of Kalinga, which led to a significant policy shift towards Dhamma?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's conquest of Kalinga in 261 BCE caused immense bloodshed, leading him to embrace Buddhism and propagate Dhamma.

questions.question: The Tamil region (the far south) during the Mauryan period was ruled by which three powerful kingdoms?
questions.explanation: The three ancient Tamil kingdoms of Chera, Chola, and Pandya existed in the far south, beyond Mauryan control.

questions.question: Which famous Greek traveller visited the court of Chandragupta Maurya?
questions.explanation: Megasthenes, a Greek ambassador from Seleucus Nicator, visited the Mauryan court and wrote the account 'Indica'.

questions.question: What was the name of the Mauryan capital city, renowned for its magnificent wooden palace?
questions.explanation: Pataliputra (modern Patna) was the capital of the Mauryan Empire, described by Megasthenes as a grand city.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler is credited with building the Grand Trunk Road (Uttarapatha) connecting the Gangetic plains to the North-West frontier?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya constructed the Uttarapatha road for administrative and trade purposes, linking his vast empire.

questions.question: The 'Saptanga' theory of state (seven organs of the state) was propounded by which ancient Indian thinker associated with the Mauryan period?
questions.explanation: Kautilya, the chief minister of Chandragupta Maurya, authored the Arthashastra which details the Saptanga theory.

questions.question: Which Tamil literary work from the Sangam period mentions the Mauryan invasion of the Tamilakam region?
questions.explanation: The Akananuru mentions the invasion of the Tamil king by the Mauryas, referring to the northern armies.

questions.question: How did Ashoka propagate his Dhamma policy throughout the empire?
questions.explanation: Ashoka inscribed his policies and moral principles on rock edicts and pillars placed across the empire in local scripts.

questions.question: What was the primary function of Mauryan officials called 'Samaharta'?
questions.explanation: The Samaharta was the chief revenue collector responsible for the state treasury and taxes in the Mauryan administration.

questions.question: The famous Mauryan sculpture, the Didarganj Yakshi, exemplifies the craftsmanship of which period?
questions.explanation: The polished stone sculpture of the Didarganj Yakshi is a classic example of Mauryan art and polished stonework.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler is credited with the conquest of Kalinga, a turning point that led to his adoption of Dhamma?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's Kalinga War (261 BCE) caused immense bloodshed, leading him to embrace Buddhism and propagate Dhamma.

questions.question: The administrative system of the Mauryan Empire is best described by which contemporary Greek source?
questions.explanation: Megasthenes, the Greek ambassador at Chandragupta's court, wrote 'Indica' detailing Mauryan administration, though the original text is lost.

questions.question: Which Tamil Sangam text mentions trade and diplomatic relations with the Mauryan Empire?
questions.explanation: Purananuru (poem 69) refers to the 'Moriyar' (Mauryas) and their war chariots, indicating contact between Tamil chieftains and the Mauryan north.

questions.question: The Mauryan Empire's decline began after the death of which ruler, leading to the rise of the Shunga dynasty?
questions.explanation: Brihadratha, the last Mauryan ruler, was assassinated by his commander Pushyamitra Shunga, who founded the Shunga dynasty around 185 BCE.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler was known as 'Amitraghata' (Slayer of Enemies) and maintained friendly relations with the Greek king Antiochus I?
questions.explanation: Bindusara, son of Chandragupta, received a diplomatic gift from Antiochus I and was called Amitraghata in Greek sources.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT a source of revenue for the Mauryan state?
questions.explanation: Jizya was a tax imposed on non-Muslims by later Islamic rulers; Mauryan taxes included bhaga, bali, and sulka as per the Arthashastra.

questions.question: The Mauryan king Ashoka's Dhamma policy emphasized which of the following principles?
options: Strict enforcement of the four varnas
questions.explanation: Ashoka's Dhamma promoted non-violence (ahimsa), religious tolerance, and welfare measures like hospitals and rest houses for all.

questions.question: Which Tamil region is believed to have been part of the Mauryan Empire under Chandragupta, as suggested by later traditions?
questions.explanation: Later Jain tradition (Bhadrabahu) suggests that Chandragupta Maurya spent his last days at Shravanabelagola, but the Pandya region had trade links; direct Mauryan rule in deep south is debated.

questions.question: The Mauryan Empire's system of provincial administration included a 'Kumara' (prince) stationed at which major city?
questions.explanation: Taxila, in the northwest, was governed by a Kumara (royal prince) due to its strategic importance for trade and defense against Greek kingdoms.

questions.question: The famous Kalinga War was fought during the reign of which Mauryan ruler?
questions.explanation: Ashoka fought the Kalinga War in 261 BCE, which led to his conversion to Buddhism.

questions.question: Which edict of Ashoka mentions the names of the five Greek kings?
questions.explanation: Rock Edict XIII of Ashoka mentions the five Greek kings (Antiochus, Ptolemy, Antigonus, Magas, Alexander) as contemporary rulers.

questions.question: What was the official language used in Ashokan inscriptions?
questions.explanation: Most Ashokan edicts were inscribed in Prakrit language using Brahmi script.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler is known for his policy of Dhamma?
questions.explanation: Ashoka propagated Dhamma (righteousness) through his edicts and appointed Dhamma Mahamattas.

questions.question: Which Tamil Nadu region is mentioned in Ashokan inscriptions?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's Rock Edicts II and XIII mention the Cholas, Pandyas, and Cheras as bordering kingdoms.

questions.question: The Mauryan art is exemplified by which famous capital?
questions.explanation: The Sarnath Lion Capital, erected by Ashoka, is the national emblem of India and a masterpiece of Mauryan art.

questions.question: Which of the following was the capital of the Mauryan Empire?
questions.explanation: Pataliputra (modern Patna) was the capital of the Mauryan Empire.

questions.question: The Mauryan administration was divided into how many provinces?
questions.explanation: The Mauryan Empire had four provinces: northern, western, southern, and eastern, each under a prince or governor.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler is known for the Rock Edicts?
questions.explanation: Ashoka issued Rock Edicts and Pillar Edicts to propagate Buddhist principles.

questions.question: The book 'Arthashastra' is associated with which Mauryan figure?
questions.explanation: Chanakya (Kautilya) wrote the Arthashastra, a treatise on statecraft and economics.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT attributed to Mauryan art?
questions.explanation: Ajanta caves were built during the Gupta period; Sanchi Stupa, Pataliputra palace, and Ashokan pillars are Mauryan.

questions.question: The decline of the Mauryan Empire was mainly due to?
questions.explanation: The decline was caused by weak successors, foreign invasions (e.g., Indo-Greeks), and economic strain.

questions.question: The Mauryan administrative system is best described in which ancient text?
questions.explanation: Kautilya's Arthashastra extensively details Mauryan statecraft, economy, and administration.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler adopted Buddhism after the Kalinga War?
questions.explanation: Ashoka embraced Buddhism following the bloody Kalinga War (261 BCE), as seen in his edicts.

questions.question: What was the title assumed by Ashoka in his inscriptions?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's edicts refer to him as 'Devanampiya Piyadasi' (Beloved of the Gods, of Gracious Mien).

questions.question: Which city served as the capital of the Mauryan Empire?
questions.explanation: Pataliputra (modern Patna) was the capital under Chandragupta and Ashoka.

questions.question: The Mauryan art is exemplified by which of the following structures?
questions.explanation: The Great Stupa at Sanchi, built during Ashoka's reign, is a key Mauryan architectural monument.

questions.question: Which Mauryan king was known as 'Amitraghata' (Slayer of Enemies) in Greek texts?
questions.explanation: Bindusara was called Amitrochates by the Greeks, meaning 'Slayer of Enemies'.

questions.question: What was the name of the provincial governor in the Mauryan administration?
questions.explanation: Provinces were often governed by royal princes (Kumara) acting as viceroys.

questions.question: The last Mauryan ruler was assassinated by his commander-in-chief, who founded which dynasty?
questions.explanation: Pushyamitra Shunga killed Brihadratha Maurya in 185 BCE and established the Shunga dynasty.

questions.question: Which Greek ambassador visited Chandragupta Maurya's court?
questions.explanation: Megasthenes, the Greek ambassador from Seleucus Nicator, wrote 'Indica' describing Mauryan administration.

questions.question: The Arthashastra, a treatise on statecraft, is attributed to:
questions.explanation: Chanakya (Kautilya) authored the Arthashastra, which outlines Mauryan political and economic policies.

questions.question: What significant change occurred in Ashoka after the Kalinga War?
questions.explanation: Horrified by the bloodshed of the Kalinga War, Ashoka converted to Buddhism and propagated Dhamma.

questions.question: From which site was the Lion Capital of Ashoka taken?
questions.explanation: The Lion Capital, now India's national emblem, was originally from the pillar at Sarnath.

questions.question: The Mauryan emperor who built the famous Sudarshan lake in Saurashtra was:
questions.explanation: Ashoka built the Sudarshan lake, which was later repaired by Rudradaman I and Skandagupta.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT a source of revenue during the Mauryan period?
questions.explanation: Ijarah was a revenue system introduced during the Mughal period, not Mauryan.
